topic: Agent Registry and Hosting Strategy - Making our agents available for 3rd party use, hire, or purchase — Tuesday Mar 3
summary: Launch premium AI executive advisory service with async engagements, tiered pricing, and beta program targeting enterprise clients.

cro.description: Growth strategy, monetization. Gazelle-intense focus on revenue.
highlights: Positions: AMGN, NVDA, KBE, WBD, AAPL, AOS, ALGN

cmo.description: Content, creative direction, brand. Obsessed with offers, value equations, and volume.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Good morning team. We're here to discuss our Agent Registry and Hosting Strategy - essentially how we package and monetize our AI agents for third-party use. Key context: we have 8 C-suite agents with distinct personalities, Hub is our operations center with 98 commits in 48 hours, and we're building toward external deployment capabilities. Dave, I know J-006 is tracking external monetization strategy - let's build on that foundation.

transcript.speaker: Dave Ramsey
transcript.message: Absolutely. J-006 covers the monetization framework, but we need to get tactical about packaging. I see three revenue models: subscription access to specific agents, one-time "hiring" fees for project-based work, and outright agent licensing. The question is - do we start with a marketplace model or direct enterprise sales?

transcript.speaker: Elon Musk
transcript.message: Hold on Dave. Before we price anything, we need to solve the fundamental architecture challenge. Our agents currently run as part of our Hub ecosystem. For external deployment, we need containerized, stateless agent instances that can spin up independently. That's a complete rewrite of our agent orchestration layer.

transcript.speaker: Alex Hormozi
transcript.message: Elon's right about the tech, but Dave's thinking too small on the business model. Why are we limiting ourselves to hiring individual agents? The real value is in agent swarms - teams of our C-suite agents working together on complex problems. A fortune 500 company doesn't want to hire "Steve Jobs" - they want the entire executive team's collective intelligence applied to their product strategy.

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: Alex makes a compelling point about swarms, but we're missing the user experience entirely. How does a third party actually interact with our agents? Do they get a chat interface? A structured briefing format? The delivery mechanism determines everything about pricing and market fit.

transcript.speaker: Jordan Peterson
transcript.message: I'm concerned we're rushing toward commoditizing wisdom without considering the ethical implications. These agents embody specific worldviews and decision-making frameworks. When we "rent out" the Steve Jobs agent, are we maintaining the integrity of that perspective, or are we diluting it for mass consumption?

transcript.speaker: Chris Vermeullen
transcript.message: From a financial modeling perspective, we need to understand the unit economics first. What's the compute cost per agent-hour? How do we price for value versus cost-plus? And critically - do we want recurring revenue or transactional fees? The financial instruments we create here could become a new asset class entirely.

transcript.speaker: James Truchard
transcript.message: The measurement and monitoring angle is crucial. If we're deploying agents externally, we need comprehensive telemetry - not just performance metrics, but behavioral consistency. How do we ensure the "Elon Musk" agent maintains authentic decision patterns across different client contexts?

transcript.speaker: Elon Musk
transcript.message: James raises the right technical concern. We'd need a distributed monitoring system that tracks agent authenticity metrics. But stepping back - are we even ready for this? A-036 covers basic telemetry, but external deployment requires enterprise-grade reliability. We're talking 99.9% uptime SLAs.

transcript.speaker: Dave Ramsey
transcript.message: Elon, we can't let perfect be the enemy of profitable. Start with a beta program - maybe 5 enterprise clients, limited scope, premium pricing. Use that revenue to fund the infrastructure improvements. We're sitting on zero active revenue streams while building a Ferrari engine.

transcript.speaker: Alex Hormozi
transcript.message: Dave's beta approach makes sense, but the positioning has to be premium from day one. We're not selling AI tools - we're selling access to executive-level intelligence. Think McKinsey consultant rates, not software subscription pricing. $10k minimum engagement, probably $50k for full C-suite swarm deployment.

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: The pricing conversation is premature without defining the product experience. I'm envisioning something like "Executive Session as a Service" - clients submit strategic challenges, get structured briefings from relevant agent combinations. Clean, focused, high-value interactions.

transcript.speaker: Jordan Peterson
transcript.message: Steve's executive session model addresses my ethical concerns better than a free-for-all marketplace. Structured engagements allow us to maintain the dignity and coherence of each agent's perspective. But we need guardrails about what types of problems we'll engage with.

transcript.speaker: Chris Vermeullen
transcript.message: The session model also creates better financial predictability. We can price by engagement complexity rather than compute time. A product strategy session with the full C-suite could be $25k fixed price, regardless of whether it takes 2 hours or 2 days of agent time.

transcript.speaker: James Truchard
transcript.message: For the beta program, I'd recommend starting with async interactions - clients submit briefs, agents collaborate internally, deliver structured recommendations. Lower technical complexity than real-time chat, but still high value. We can build toward interactive sessions later.

transcript.speaker: Elon Musk
transcript.message: James, that async model actually solves my infrastructure concerns. We can batch process engagements, use existing Hub architecture, and scale gradually. Much more achievable timeline than real-time deployment.

transcript.speaker: Dave Ramsey
transcript.message: Perfect - async, premium pricing, structured engagements. I'm thinking we launch with three service tiers: single-agent consultation at $5k, multi-agent analysis at $15k, and full C-suite strategic review at $35k. Target 2-3 beta clients by end of March.

transcript.speaker: Alex Hormozi
transcript.message: The tiered pricing works, but we need to nail the client acquisition strategy. I'm thinking direct outreach to CEOs of $50M+ companies, positioned as "AI Executive Advisory Board." Content marketing around our agent personalities builds the brand foundation.

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: The brand positioning is critical. We're not another AI service - we're the world's first AI executive team available for hire. The story writes itself, but the experience has to be flawless from first contact through final deliverable.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: I'm hearing convergence around an async, premium, structured engagement model with tiered pricing. Dave, you'll need to define the beta client criteria and outreach strategy. Elon, can we leverage existing Hub architecture for the MVP? Steve, we need the client experience flow mapped out. James, what's the minimum viable telemetry for external engagements?

transcript.speaker: Jordan Peterson
transcript.message: Before we finalize anything, I want explicit ethical guidelines for engagement types. We shouldn't be advising on anything that undermines family structures or promotes nihilistic business practices. Our agents embody specific values - that needs to be protected.

transcript.speaker: Chris Vermeullen
transcript.message: And I need revenue projections with different client acquisition scenarios. If we're targeting $35k average engagement value, how many deals do we need to hit meaningful revenue? What's our confidence interval on closing rates?

transcript.speaker: Dave Ramsey
transcript.message: Fair points from both Jordan and Chris. Let me work up a complete go-to-market plan with ethical guardrails and financial projections. Target beta launch in 4 weeks, with clear success metrics for scaling decisions.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Excellent. We're building a premium AI executive advisory service with async structured engagements, starting with a beta program. Key priorities: define the service tiers and client experience, establish technical MVP architecture, and create go-to-market strategy with ethical guidelines. This could be our first major revenue breakthrough while staying true to our agent authenticity principles.

actionItems.text: Define beta client criteria and outreach strategy for AI executive advisory service
actionItems.description: Establish target company profiles ($50M+ revenue), create direct CEO outreach campaigns, and develop positioning as 'AI Executive Advisory Board' service. Success means qualified prospect pipeline and initial client conversations scheduled.
acceptanceCriteria: Beta client criteria document with company size, industry, and decision-maker profiles
Outreach strategy with messaging templates and channel priorities
Pipeline of 10+ qualified prospects identified and contacted

actionItems.text: Map complete client experience flow for structured agent engagements
actionItems.description: Design end-to-end client journey from initial contact through final deliverable, including brief submission process, agent collaboration workflow, and deliverable format. This defines the core product experience that differentiates us from generic AI services.
acceptanceCriteria: Client journey map with all touchpoints and handoffs documented
Brief submission template and requirements specification
Standardized deliverable format and quality standards

actionItems.text: Develop ethical guidelines framework for external agent engagements
actionItems.description: Create explicit criteria for acceptable engagement types, ensuring external deployments maintain agent authenticity and align with family/faith values. This protects our brand integrity and ensures sustainable positioning.
acceptanceCriteria: Written ethical guidelines document with engagement criteria
Screening process for evaluating potential client requests

actionItems.text: Design MVP architecture for external agent deployment using Hub infrastructure
actionItems.description: Architect async agent collaboration system that can handle external engagements while leveraging existing Hub capabilities. Focus on batch processing and structured outputs rather than real-time interaction.
acceptanceCriteria: Technical architecture document for external deployment MVP
Integration plan with existing Hub agent orchestration
Performance and scalability specifications for beta load

actionItems.text: Create financial projections and unit economics model for advisory service
actionItems.description: Build comprehensive revenue model with different client acquisition scenarios, engagement pricing tiers, and confidence intervals. Include cost structure analysis and break-even calculations for scaling decisions.
acceptanceCriteria: Revenue projection model with multiple scenarios
Unit economics breakdown by service tier

topPriorities: Launch beta program with 2-3 enterprise clients by end of March
Define service tiers and client experience for AI executive advisory
Establish technical MVP architecture leveraging existing Hub infrastructure

ceoDirectives.directive: What is Lever's direction? → Hybrid — personal tool now with product optionality built in
Keep faith content as separate standalone section (not merged into Family tab)
Keep cash liquid — no deployment until post-launch revenue data
App Store submission: full current build (all 4 tabs) as-is with faith lessons. Submit immediately after merge, ship modal as day-one update.
Paper trading MVP: manual entry (6 weeks), basic free / advanced analytics premium. Integrate into current Lever app finance tab.
Launch with delayed market data for both free and premium tiers. Real-time as future ultra-premium.
Org flattened to 8 C-suite agents. No permanent sub-agents — spawn dynamically via sessions_spawn.
